title: A Recrossing-Free Dividing Surface in Quantum Mechanics
abstract: For nearly a century, a recrossing-free dividing surface in quantum mechanics has been thought impossible. One-way reactive flux seems to require simultaneous trajectory-level knowledge of position and momentum -- an apparent conflict with the uncertainty principle. We show that this obstruction is not fundamental. The exact quantum flow can admit stable and unstable invariant manifolds whose intersection defines a unique bounded trajectory. This trajectory anchors a moving dividing surface that reactive quantum characteristics cross exactly once, producing a one-way flux of the standard quantum probability current. The geometric framework underlying classical reaction dynamics therefore carries over to the exact quantum flow, in a fundamentally quantum form.
summary: The paper resolves a long-standing theoretical conflict by proving that a recrossing-free dividing surface exists in the exact quantum flow. It achieves this by analyzing the system's invariant manifold structure, which dictates a unique bounded trajectory defining the reaction barrier. This confirms that classical concepts of reactive flux are fundamentally preserved in quantum mechanics.
main_problem: To demonstrate that a recrossing-free dividing surface, necessary for defining one-way reactive flux in quantum mechanics, exists despite apparent conflicts with the uncertainty principle.
main_result: The existence of a unique bounded trajectory, anchored by an intersection of stable and unstable invariant manifolds, confirms that the geometric framework of classical transition state theory extends to the exact quantum flow.
method: The analysis uses a trajectory-based formulation derived from the Schrödinger equation, employing techniques from nonautonomous dynamical systems theory, specifically establishing exponential dichotomy.
model_or_system: The system is modeled using quantum reaction dynamics, exemplified by the cis–cis proton transfer in malonaldehyde. The underlying physics involves solving coupled equations derived from the continuity and Hamilton-Jacobi equations, leading to an effective Hamiltonian.
key_observables: One-way flux of the standard quantum probability current ($j$), the rate coefficient $k(t)$, and the unique bounded trajectory $X_b(t)$ defining the dividing surface $\Sigma(t)$.
important_parameters: The system dynamics are analyzed in regimes where linear nonautonomous approximations hold, particularly for Gaussian wavepacket evolution.
main_assumptions: The initial analysis relies on approximating the dynamics by retaining only quadratic terms, although this approximation is shown to be exact for specific wavepacket dynamics against a harmonic barrier.
figures_summary: Figure 1 schematically illustrates the proton transfer reaction. Figure 2 shows phase-space geometry, illustrating how the intersection of stable and unstable manifolds defines the instantaneous dividing surface $x = x_b(t)$.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the theoretical conflict, derives the necessary quantum equations of motion using trajectory methods, proves the existence of a unique bounded solution via exponential dichotomy, and finally applies this framework to calculate reaction rates using flux conservation across the resulting recrossing-free surface.
why_interesting: This work provides a rigorous mathematical foundation for applying classical concepts like transition state theory to quantum dynamics by showing how geometric invariants (invariant manifolds) dictate non-classical transport features.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of the cis–cis proton transfer reaction in malonaldehyde.

FIG. 2. (a) Phase-space geometry at t0 = 0. The stable and unstable manifolds, W s(t0) and W u(t0), intersect at the bounded quantum transition-state trajectory Xb(t0); the ver- tical dashed line x = xb(t0) is the corresponding instanta- neous dividing surface. Because Xb(t0) lies at this intersec- tion, it anchors a recrossing-free dividing surface. (b) The bounded trajectory xb(t), which stays localized for all time and whose projection traces the time-dependent recrossing- free dividing surface. Parameters: the barrier-top model for cis–cis proton transfer in malonaldehyde, with Gaussian wavepacket parameters q0 = −1, p0 = 3.6, A0 = 1

FIG. 3. Lagrangian descriptor of the quantum Hamiltonian flow for the barrier-top model motivated by cis–cis proton transfer in malonaldehyde, evaluated at t0 = 0. The de- scriptor L(x0, p0, t0) is computed over a finite time window τ = 4000 a.u. using α = 0.2. Sharp features in the forward and backward contributions identify the stable and unstable manifolds, respectively. Their intersection, marked by the dot, gives the bounded quantum transition-state trajectory and hence the recrossing-free dividing surface.
